title: 10 best herbal tinctures for queasy stomach
intro_desc: Herbal tinctures for queasy stomach are concentrated extracts of medicinal herbs, prepared by soaking plant material in a liquid solvent such as alcohol or glycerin. They are an effective natural remedy for alleviating queasy stomach due to their ability to soothe the digestive system and reduce inflammation. Examples of herbal tinctures that help with queasy stomach include ginger, chamomile, and peppermint. Ginger, for instance, is well-known for its anti-inflammatory and anti-nausea properties, making it an excellent choice for treating motion sickness or morning sickness during pregnancy. Chamomile, on the other hand, has calming effects on the stomach and can help reduce acid reflux, while peppermint is known to relax the gastrointestinal muscles and improve digestion. By using herbal tinctures, individuals can find relief from queasy stomach symptoms, leading to improved appetite, increased energy levels, and a better quality of life.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mugwort tincture helps with queasy stomach because it contains anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can soothe an upset stomach and alleviate symptoms of nausea. Mugwort has been used traditionally to aid digestion, stimulate appetite, and relieve gastric spasms. Its active compounds, such as volatile oils, flavonoids, and alkaloids, work together to regulate gut motility, reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, and promote overall digestive health. As a result, mugwort tincture can provide fast-acting relief for a queasy stomach, making it a popular natural remedy for digestive discomfort.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic property: Mugwort tincture can help relax the smooth muscles of the digestive tract, reducing stomach spasms and cramps that can cause queasiness. This is due to the presence of volatile oils like camphor, camphene, and cineole.

Carminative property: Mugwort tincture aids in expelling gas from the stomach and intestines, which can alleviate feelings of bloating and discomfort associated with a queasy stomach. This is attributed to the presence of flavonoids and terpenoids.

Choleretic property: Mugwort tincture stimulates the production of bile in the liver, which can improve digestion and reduce the likelihood of a queasy stomach. This is due to the presence of lactone compounds like thujone and artemisinin.

Nervine property: Mugwort tincture has a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help reduce anxiety and tension that can exacerbate a queasy stomach. This is due to the presence of flavonoids and essential oils.

Antibacterial property: Mugwort tincture can help kill harmful bacteria in the digestive tract that can cause stomach upset and queasiness. This is due to the presence of volatile oils like camphene, 1,8-cineole, and alpha-thujone.
remedy_parts: Rhizomes: Mugwort rhizomes are commonly used to make medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs due to their anti-inflammatory and calming properties.
Leaves: The leaves of the mugwort plant are often used in tinctures because of their ability to soothe the digestive system and alleviate symptoms of nausea.
Flowers: Mugwort flowers are used in tinctures for their carminative effects, which help to expel gas from the stomach and alleviate queasiness.
Seeds: Mugwort seeds are used to make medicinal tinctures due to their ability to stimulate the digestive system and reduce symptoms of nausea and vomiting.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh mugwort leaves and flowers in the early morning, ensuring they are free of pesticides and other contaminants.
Chop the harvested plant material into small pieces to increase surface area for efficient extraction.
Combine one part mugwort plant material with two parts 80-proof vodka or grain alcohol in a clean glass jar, filling it to the brim.
Store the jar in a cool dark place for six to eight weeks, shaking daily to facilitate extraction and infusion.
Strain the liquid through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a separate container, discarding the solids and reserving the tincture.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peruvian bark tincture helps with queasy stomach because it contains natural alkaloids, such as quinine and cinchonine, which have been shown to have antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties. These compounds can help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing cramping and discomfort. Additionally, Peruvian bark has been used traditionally to treat digestive issues, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Its bitter taste can also stimulate the production of digestive juices, improving digestion and absorption of nutrients. Overall, Peruvian bark tincture can provide relief for a queasy stomach by reducing inflammation, relaxing muscles, and promoting healthy digestion.
remedy_properties: Anti-emetic: Peruvian bark tincture contains alkaloids such as cinchonine and cinchonidine that help reduce nausea and vomiting, providing relief for queasy stomach.

Prokinetic: The active constituents in Peruvian bark tincture, including tannins and saponins, stimulate gastric motility, helping to move food through the digestive tract and alleviate queasy stomach.

Anti-inflammatory: The presence of alkaloids in Peruvian bark tincture, such as quinine, reduces inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, providing relief from discomfort and irritation associated with queasy stomach.
Analgesic: Peruvian bark tincture contains alkaloids with pain-relieving properties, such as quinine and cinchonine, which can help alleviate abdominal pain and cramping caused by queasy stomach.
Astringent: The tannins in Peruvian bark tincture have astringent properties that can help reduce secretions and soothe mucous membranes, providing relief for queasy stomach symptoms.
remedy_parts: Bark: The bark of the Peruvian bark plant is commonly used to make medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs due to its alkaloid content, particularly the compound called cinchonine, which has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and muscle-relaxing properties that can help soothe an upset stomach.

Roots: The roots of the Peruvian bark plant are also used in medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs because they contain high levels of quinine, which has been shown to have antimalarial and muscle-relaxing properties that can help alleviate stomach discomfort.

Leaves: The leaves of the Peruvian bark plant are used in medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs due to their tannin content, which has astringent properties that can help reduce inflammation and soothe an upset stomach.

Flowers: The flowers of the Peruvian bark plant are sometimes used in medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs because they contain flavonoids and other compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation.

Seeds: The seeds of the Peruvian bark plant are not typically used in medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs, as they contain lower levels of the active compounds found in the bark, roots, and leaves.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh cinchona officinalis bark in the morning after dew has dried.
Dry the harvested bark in a warm and airy place for at least seven days.
Chop one part of dried cinchona officinalis bark into small pieces using a mortar and pestle.
Steep 1:10 ratio of chopped bark to high-proof vodka or ethanol for twelve hours in a glass jar.
Strain the liquid through cheesecloth or a coffee filter and transfer to dark glass bottles.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice tincture helps with queasy stomach because it contains active compounds that can help soothe digestive issues. The main component, glycyrrhizin, has been shown to increase mucus production in the gastrointestinal tract, which can protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation. This can help alleviate symptoms of nausea and indigestion, making licorice tincture a popular natural remedy for an upset stomach. Additionally, licorice tincture may help stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, further aiding in the digestion process and reducing the likelihood of queasiness.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Licorice tincture can reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, which is important for queasy stomach as it can help alleviate symptoms of gastritis and acid reflux. The active constituents responsible for this property are glycyrrhizin and flavonoids.

Demulcent: Licorice tincture can form a protective film on the stomach lining, which is important for queasy stomach as it can help soothe and protect the stomach from irritants and acid. The active constituent responsible for this property is glycyrrhizin.

Mucosal healing: Licorice tincture can promote the healing of the stomach lining, which is important for queasy stomach as it can help treat and prevent stomach ulcers. The active constituents responsible for this property are glycyrrhizin and flavonoids.

Antispasmodic: Licorice tincture can relieve smooth muscle spasms, which is important for queasy stomach as it can help reduce stomach cramps and pain. The active constituent responsible for this property is glycyrrhetinic acid.

Antiemetic: Licorice tincture can help reduce nausea and vomiting, which is important for queasy stomach as it can help alleviate symptoms of motion sickness, food poisoning, and other digestive disorders. The active constituents responsible for this property are glycyrrhizin and flavonoids.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, which can help soothe an upset stomach and reduce inflammation.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the licorice plant, similar to the roots, have anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate symptoms of a queasy stomach.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant contain flavonoids that can help reduce stomach spasms and calm an upset stomach.
Seeds: The seeds of the licorice plant have been used in traditional medicine to treat stomach ailments, including nausea and heartburn.
Flowers: The flowers of the licorice plant contain antioxidants that can help protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots in large quantities for processing.
Chop the dried roots into small pieces to increase their surface area for extraction.
Combine the chopped roots with vodka or high-proof grain alcohol at a 1:5 ratio by weight for menstruum preparation.
Steep the mixture in a glass container for 2-4 weeks, shaking daily, allowing solubles to extract from root material.
Filter and strain the liquid using cheesecloth or coffee filters before transferring it to dark glass bottles.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St. John's wort tincture helps with queasy stomach because it contains antispasmodic properties that can help soothe and relax the digestive tract, reducing symptoms of nausea and stomach discomfort. This herbal remedy has been used for centuries to treat various gastrointestinal issues, including indigestion, cramps, and gastritis. The active compounds in St. John's wort, such as hypericin and hyperforin, are believed to help regulate the production of stomach acid, reducing the likelihood of acid reflux and other related symptoms. Additionally, St. John's wort has been shown to have calming effects on the nervous system, which can help alleviate stress-related digestive issues and promote overall digestive health.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic: St. John's wort tincture helps relax the smooth muscles of the digestive tract, reducing stomach cramps and spasms that can cause queasiness. This property is due to the presence of active constituents like hypericin and hyperforin.

Anti-inflammatory: The tincture can reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, alleviating discomfort and irritation that may contribute to queasy stomach. The active compounds responsible for this property include hypericin, hyperforin, and flavonoids.

Anxiolytic: St. John's wort tincture has calming effects that can help reduce anxiety and stress, which are often associated with queasy stomach. Hypericin and hyperforin are the primary active constituents responsible for this anxiolytic effect.

Nausea reduction: St. John's wort tincture can directly help reduce feelings of nausea, providing relief for queasy stomach. The active constituents responsible for this property include hypericin, hyperforin, and flavonoids.

Digestive stimulant: The tincture can help stimulate the digestive process, promoting better nutrient absorption and reducing the likelihood of queasy stomach. Active constituents like hypericin, hyperforin, and essential oils contribute to this digestive stimulant property.
remedy_parts: Flowers: St. John's Wort flowers are commonly used in medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs due to their anti-inflammatory and mild sedative properties.
Leaves: The leaves of St. John's Wort contain active compounds that help regulate digestion, making them suitable for addressing queasy stomachs.
Aerial parts: While not a specific part, aerial parts of St. John's Wort, which include stems and leaves, are sometimes used together in tinctures for their calming effects on the digestive system.
Buds: St. John's Wort buds are rich in essential oils and antioxidants, providing soothing relief for an upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality fresh or dried st john's wort leaves and flowers at dawn on a sunny day.
Cleanly sort and chop the plant material into small pieces and remove any stems or debris.
Combine 10 parts of chopped plant material with 20% vodka or 35% grain alcohol in a clean glass jar.
Steep the mixture for 2-4 weeks shaking daily to ensure all plant constituents are extracted into the solvent.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or coffee filter and store it in dark glass bottles at room temperature.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint tincture helps with queasy stomach because it contains menthol, a compound that has antispasmodic properties, which can help relax the gastrointestinal tract and reduce inflammation. This can alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome, such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Additionally, peppermint has been shown to improve the flow of bile, which can aid in digestion and further help to settle an upset stomach. Its cooling and calming effects can also help to soothe the stomach and provide rapid relief from discomfort.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic: Peppermint tincture can help relax gastrointestinal muscles, reducing spasms and cramping that can contribute to a queasy stomach. This property is due to the presence of menthol and menthone.

Carminative: Peppermint tincture can help alleviate gas and bloating, which can cause or worsen a queasy stomach. This is attributed to the menthol content, which promotes the expulsion of intestinal gas.

Analgesic: Peppermint tincture can help relieve pain, including abdominal pain associated with a queasy stomach. Menthol and other volatile oils in peppermint have analgesic properties that can help manage discomfort.
Anesthetic: Peppermint tincture can help numb the stomach lining, reducing the sensation of nausea. Menthol has anesthetic properties that can help alleviate the queasy feeling.

Choleretic: Peppermint tincture can help stimulate bile production, aiding in digestion and reducing the likelihood of a queasy stomach. This is facilitated by the menthol and menthone content, which encourage bile flow.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Peppermint leaves are commonly used in medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs due to their calming and soothing properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the peppermint plant have antispasmodic properties, making them useful for alleviating symptoms of queasy stomach.
Seeds: Peppermint seeds contain essential oils that can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, providing relief for queasy stomach.
remedy_recipe: Harvest peppermint leaves at dawn on a sunny day when the dew has dried.
Rinse the harvested peppermint leaves with cold filtered water and pat dry gently.
Chop 1 part fresh peppermint leaves and combine with 1 part high-proof vodka in a clean glass jar.
Store the jar in a cool dark place for 6-8 weeks to allow infusion of essential oils.
Strain the tincture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle after 6-8 weeks and discard solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Caraway tincture helps with queasy stomach because it contains anti-spasmodic and carminative properties that can provide relief from digestive discomforts such as stomach cramps, bloating, and gas. The active compounds in caraway, particularly carvol and carvone, work to relax the gastrointestinal muscles and reduce muscle spasms, allowing for the smooth passage of food through the digestive system. This can help alleviate the sensation of queasiness and promote a more comfortable digestive process. Additionally, caraway has been shown to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, further supporting healthy digestion and reducing the likelihood of an upset stomach.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic property: Caraway tincture can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing stomach cramps and discomfort, important for queasy stomach as it alleviates one of the main causes of nausea. This property is attributed to the compound carvol.

Carminative property: Caraway tincture aids in expelling gas from the digestive tract, helping to relieve bloating and flatulence, important for queasy stomach as it can reduce uncomfortable sensations in the stomach. This property is due to the presence of volatile oils, particularly carvone and limonene.

Stomachic property: Caraway tincture stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, improving digestion and nutrient absorption, important for queasy stomach as it promotes overall gastrointestinal health. This property is attributed to the compound carvone.

Anxiolytic property: Caraway tincture can help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation, important for queasy stomach as stress and anxiety can exacerbate nausea and other digestive symptoms. This property is due to the presence of volatile oils, particularly limonene and carvone.

Antiemetic property: Caraway tincture can help alleviate feelings of nausea and prevent vomiting, important for queasy stomach as it directly addresses the main symptom. This property is attributed to the compound carvone.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Caraway seeds are commonly used in medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs due to their carminative and antispasmodic properties, which help to relieve bloating, gas, and cramps.

Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the caraway plant contain volatile oils that have been shown to aid digestion and reduce stomach discomfort, making them an effective ingredient in medicinal tinctures for queasy stomachs.

Leaves: The leaves of the caraway plant have been used in traditional medicine to soothe digestive issues, including queasy stomachs, due to their calming and anti-inflammatory effects on the gastrointestinal tract.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality carum carvi seeds, carefully selecting those with a strong aroma and no signs of mold.
Dry the harvested seeds in a single layer, spreading them out on paper bags or trays for 7-10 days.
Grind the dried seeds into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle, processing 1 part seed to 4 parts carrier oil.
Mix the ground seed powder with high-proof vodka or everclear in a glass jar, using a ratio of 1:2 (seed powder:alcohol) for a minimum of 6 weeks.
Strain the tincture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into dark glass bottles, discarding the solids and labeling the tincture with ingredients, date made, and expiration date.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried foeniculum vulgare leaves and stems, ensuring at least 2 pounds of material for the desired yield.
Combine the harvested plant material with 50% vodka by volume in a clean glass jar, covering the contents completely.
Store the jar in a cool dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking daily to facilitate solvent extraction and maceration.
Strain the liquid through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean container, discarding the solids and retaining the tincture.
Transfer the tincture to amber glass bottles, labeling and storing in a cool dark place for up to 2 years without refrigeration.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh spearmint leaves mentha spicata and wash them gently with pure water.
Chop the washed spearmint leaves into small pieces using sharp kitchen scissors or knives.

Store the mixture in a cool dark place for 6 weeks to allow for maceration and extraction.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh zingiber officinale roots, selecting those with no signs of mold or mildew for use in the tincture process.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Garden angelica tincture helps with queasy stomach because it contains beneficial properties that can soothe and calm an upset stomach. This herb, also known as Archangelica officinalis, has been used traditionally for its carminative and stomachic effects, which can help to reduce bloating, gas, and nausea. remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: This property helps reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, which can cause queasiness. The active constituents responsible for this property are a group of compounds called furanocoumarins.

Carminative property: This property helps to expel gas from the stomach and intestines, thereby alleviating queasiness. The primary active constituents responsible for this property are the essential oils found in garden angelica, such as limonene and α-pinene.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh angelica archangelica roots and leaves during their peak growth season for optimal potency.
Cleanse the harvested plant material by gently washing with cool running water to remove dirt and debris.
Chop the cleaned plant material into small pieces and combine with a 70% vodka or grain alcohol at a ratio of 1:5 by weight.
Allow the mixture to steep in a cool dark place for 2-4 weeks, shaking daily, to extract the medicinal properties.
Strain the liquid through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle, discarding the solids and storing the tincture in the refrigerator for up to 2 years.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat queasy stomach with herbal tinctures is by using ginger or peppermint tinctures. Ginger has been shown to alleviate digestive issues, including nausea and vomiting. Peppermint, on the other hand, has antispasmodic properties that can help relax the gastrointestinal tract, reducing symptoms of queasiness. To use, mix 20-30 drops of either tincture in a small amount of water and sip slowly. Repeat as needed every few hours, but avoid taking large amounts as it can lead to heartburn or diarrhea. Always consult with a healthcare professional before starting any new treatment.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to queasy stomach that are treated with herbal tinctures include indigestion, nausea, and motion sickness. Ginger is a popular herb used to alleviate queasiness and vomiting, often taken as a tincture. Peppermint is another herb that aids in digestion and reduces symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome. Chamomile is known for its calming properties and can help soothe an upset stomach and reduce inflammation. Licorice root can also help treat stomach ulcers and reduce acid reflux. However, it is important to consult with a healthcare provider before taking any herbal remedies, especially for those who are pregnant, breastfeeding, or have underlying health conditions.
